---
title: "Data and Privacy"
---

# Data and Privacy

Mayon is designed around a local-first principle. Your data stays on your machine. This page explains how storage, API keys, and offline capabilities work.

## Storage

All application data lives in a single SQLite database.

### Browser (OPFS)

In the browser, the database is `file:mayon.sqlite` stored in the **Origin Private File System** (OPFS). OPFS is a browser API that provides a high-performance, sandboxed filesystem available only to the originating site. Data persists across page reloads and browser restarts.

SQLite runs as WebAssembly inside a Web Worker so that database operations do not block the main UI thread.

The database is scoped to the origin (`https://your-domain.com`). Clearing site data in the browser removes the database.

### Desktop (native SQLite)

The desktop app stores its database as `sqlite:mayon.db` in the Tauri application data directory. The path depends on the operating system:

- **Windows:** `%APPDATA%\com.mayon.app\`
- **macOS:** `~/Library/Application Support/com.mayon.app/`
- **Linux:** `~/.config/com.mayon.app/`

SQLite runs in WAL (Write-Ahead Logging) mode for better concurrent read performance. The file persists across app restarts and updates.

### Migrations

Both runtimes run the same drizzle migrations at boot. Migrations are bundled into the SPA build as TypeScript — no filesystem access is needed at runtime. If the database is empty (first run), all migrations apply sequentially. If it already exists, only new migrations run.

## API key storage

API keys are never written to the SQLite `settings` table. The settings table holds only provider configuration metadata (base URL, model, template type).

**Desktop:** Keys are stored in the operating system's keychain. On macOS this is Keychain Access, on Windows it is Credential Manager, and on Linux it is the Secret Service (via `gnome-keyring` or `kwallet`). The Rust `keyring` crate handles storage. The key plaintext never enters the webview — the Tauri bridge only exposes `key_has`, `key_set`, and `key_delete` commands.

**Browser:** Keys are stored in IndexedDB, which is scoped to the same origin as the app. The key is never transmitted anywhere except the provider's API endpoint during streaming.

If you upgrade from a version that stored keys in the `settings` table, a one-time `migrateLegacyKeys()` step on boot moves them to the runtime key store and removes the old rows.

## No telemetry

Mayon does not collect, transmit, or report any telemetry. There are no analytics, no usage tracking, and no phone-home mechanisms. The app does not make any network requests except to the AI providers you explicitly configure.

## Offline capability

After the first load the app is fully offline-capable. The SPA is served as static files. The database is local. Chat, branching, labs, and quizzes all work without an internet connection.

The only feature that requires network access is AI streaming (sending prompts to a provider and receiving responses). If you use a local provider like Ollama, even AI streaming works offline.
